did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) methods for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it employed ML classifiers (ANN, KNN, SVM, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree) with GLCM feature extraction, and DL models including CNNs and pretrained networks (AlexNet, VGG-16, VGG-19, EfficientNet-b0, ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, DenseNet-201, Inception-v3, MobileNet-v2). It also proposed two specific methods: KNN with pretrained deep neural networks as feature extractors (KNN-PDNN) and AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (AlexGWO). These were tested on 4000 dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset to classify benign and malignant tumors.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors proposed two new methods (KNN-PDNN and AlexGWO) for skin cancer classification. It details specific machine learning and deep learning models tested on the ISIC dataset (4000 images), reports quantitative results (accuracy exceeding 99%), and includes sections on materials, methods, experimental results, and discussion, which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Performance Enhancement of Skin Cancer Classification Using Computer Vision
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification by proposing two computer vision methods to distinguish between benign and malignant dermoscopic images. The researchers utilized a dataset of 4000 images from the ISIC archive, split into 80% for training and 20% for testing. They evaluated various machine learning classifiers with GLCM features and deep learning models, including ten pretrained CNNs. Two novel approaches were introduced: KNN with pretrained deep neural networks as feature extractors (KNN-PDNN) and AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (AlexGWO). The results demonstrated that the proposed methods outperformed existing approaches, with KNN-PDNN exceeding 99% accuracy in some models and AlexGWO achieving 99% accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant tumors; 2000 images used in the dataset.
Classified as benign tumors; 2000 images used in the dataset. The paper groups non-melanoma lesions under the benign category for binary classification.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant skin cancer diagnosis
all_task_types.details: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign (non-melanoma) and malignant (melanoma) tumor categories. The study evaluates machine learning classifiers (ANN, KNN, SVM, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree) with GLCM features and deep learning models (CNNs, pretrained networks like AlexNet, VGG-16, etc.) for this purpose. Accuracy metrics (exceeding 99%) are reported.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of 4000 dermoscopic images selected from the ISIC archive, an open-source platform with publicly available dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The images are in JPEG format.

use.training: Used for training machine learning classifiers (ANN, KNN, SVM, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree) with GLCM features and deep learning models (CNNs, pretrained networks like AlexNet, VGG-16, etc.).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification accuracy of the proposed methods (KNN-PDNN and AlexGWO) and compared models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score) and qualitative comparisons stating that proposed methods 'outperformed' others.

ISIC Archive Dataset.dataset_description: 4000 dermoscopic images (2000 benign, 2000 malignant), split into 80% training (3200 images) and 20% testing (800 images).
ISIC Archive Dataset.evaluation_setting: Binary classification of benign vs. malignant skin cancer.

Machine Learning (with GLCM features).performance_summary: Reported to give lower accuracies compared to deep learning methods. Specific metric values not provided in the text.
Machine Learning (with GLCM features).notes: Used 5-fold cross-validation. Extracted 26 features per image using GLCM.

Deep Learning (Pretrained CNNs).performance_summary: Reported to provide high performance. Specific metric values for individual models not provided in the text.
Deep Learning (Pretrained CNNs).notes: Input size resized to 224x224 for these models.

Proposed Method 1 (KNN-PDNN).description: KNN classifier using pretrained deep neural networks as feature extractors.
Proposed Method 1 (KNN-PDNN).notes: Specific models achieving >99% are not listed individually in the provided text.

Proposed Method 2 (AlexGWO).description: AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for hyperparameter tuning.

ISIC Archive Dataset.notes: The paper states that the proposed methods (KNN-PDNN and AlexGWO) outperformed the other tested approaches. No specific p-values, confidence intervals, or standard deviations were reported.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple architectures tested: Machine Learning classifiers (ANN, KNN, SVM, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree) with GLCM features; Deep Learning models including custom CNN, pretrained CNNs (AlexNet, VGG-16, VGG-19, EfficientNet-b0, ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, DenseNet-201, Inception-v3, MobileNet-v2); Proposed methods: KNN-PDNN (KNN with pretrained DNN feature extractors) and AlexGWO (AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer).
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained models were loaded from large-scale image-classification jobs (e.g., ImageNet). Weights of early layers were frozen (learning rates set to zero) during transfer learning. For KNN-PDNN, pretrained deep neural networks were used as feature extractors.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (for CNN and pretrained models). Images were resized using augmented image data store.

custom_cnn.layers: Five convolutional layers (32, 32, 64, 128, 128 filters), one fully connected layer, one dropout layer, five maximum pooling layers, five ReLU layers, one softmax layer, five batch normalization layers.
custom_cnn.normalization: Batch normalization before each ReLU layer

alexnet.description: CNN model with stacked convolutional layers, max pooling, dropout, information growth, ReLU activations, and stochastic gradient descent with force.

resnet.variants: ResNet-18 (18 layers), ResNet-50 (50 layers), ResNet-101 (101 layers).
resnet.structure: Residual blocks with two or three convolutional layers per block.

densenet.structure: Dense connections where each layer receives aggregate data from previous layers. Includes pooling layer and bottleneck development.

vgg.structure: Five blocks of 3x3 convolutional layers, max pooling (2x2 kernel, stride 2), two fully connected layers with 4096 nodes and ReLU activation, final layer with 1000 nodes and softmax.

efficientnet.structure: Built on EfficientNet-B0, adapts inverted bottleneck residual block MobileNetV2 (MBConv). Scales width, height, and resolution.

mobilenet.structure: Shortcut connections, inverted residual blocks, bottleneck blocks. 3.5 million parameters, 14 MB size.

inception.structure: Inception module computing 1x1, 3x3, and 5x5 convolutions. Outputs layered on top of each other.

ml_classifiers.SVM: Support Vector Machine with hyperplane separation.
ml_classifiers.KNN: K-Nearest Neighbor with 10 nearest neighbors, Euclidean distance.
ml_classifiers.NB: Naïve Bayes based on Bayes' probability theorem.

proposed_knn_pdnn.description: KNN classifier using features extracted from the global pooling layer of pretrained deep neural networks.
proposed_knn_pdnn.feature_extraction: Global pooling layer activation used to acquire feature representations.

proposed_alexgwo.description: AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for hyperparameter tuning.
proposed_alexgwo.optimizer: Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) based on hunting concept (alpha, beta, delta, omega wolves).

training_details.augmentation: Random vertical flipping, random translation up to 30 pixels, scaling up to 10% horizontally and vertically.

optimization.alexgwo: Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for hyperparameter optimization.

training_details.transfer_learning: Early layers frozen (learning rates set to zero). Classification layer replaced with new layer without class labels. Fine-tuned network used for classification.
training_details.epochs: Not explicitly specified for all models, but noted that fewer epochs are needed when using transfer learning.

classification_or_output_head.details: For pretrained models, the original classification layer (e.g., 1000 nodes for VGG) was replaced with a new layer without class labels, followed by a final classification layer specifying the network's output classes. For KNN-PDNN, KNN classifier was fitted on extracted features.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 250 × 250 pixels to handle different dimensions
Filtering images using a median filter
Cropping images to 150 × 150 pixels to remove black borders from the microscope
Segmentation of the lesion region (Note: This step was initially considered but ultimately declined/rejected because it negatively affected classification accuracy)
Resizing segmented images back to 250 × 250 pixels
Data augmentation for training images including random vertical flipping, random translation up to 30 pixels, and scaling up to 10% horizontally and vertically
Automatic resizing of training images to 224 × 224 pixels for pretrained CNN models using an augmented image data store

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images to prevent overfitting.
Applied to training images with a range of up to 30 pixels.
Applied to training images with a range of up to 10% horizontally and vertically.
Used via an augmented image data store to resize training images to 224x224 pixels for input into CNN and pretrained models.

conclusions: The proposed KNN-PDNN method (K-nearest neighbor classifier using pretrained deep neural networks as feature extractors) outperformed other tested approaches, achieving accuracy exceeding 99% in some models.
The proposed AlexGWO method (AlexNet optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer for hyperparameter tuning) achieved 99% accuracy.
Machine learning methods (ANN, KNN, SVM, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree) with GLCM feature extraction yielded lower accuracies compared to deep learning methods.

Deep learning approaches, including custom CNNs and ten pretrained networks (AlexNet, VGG-16, VGG-19, EfficientNet-b0, ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, DenseNet-201, Inception-v3, MobileNet-v2), provided high performance.
The implementation of the proposed computer vision methods allows for early detection and expedited diagnosis of melanoma, which can enable more efficient treatment and reduce mortality rates.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly curated, balanced subset of the ISIC dataset (4000 images: 2000 benign, 2000 malignant) rather than the full, imbalanced archive, which may limit generalizability to real-world clinical populations with different class distributions.

The authors explicitly rejected lesion segmentation because it negatively affected classification accuracy, suggesting that including background context might be beneficial for this specific task or that segmentation errors introduced noise.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance claims are based on descriptive metrics and qualitative comparisons.
The paper does not report standard deviations or variance for the reported accuracy metrics (e.g., >99% for KNN-PDNN, 99% for AlexGWO), making it difficult to assess the stability of the results.

The dataset split is a simple random split (80% training, 20% testing) without mention of patient-level separation, raising potential concerns about data leakage if multiple images from the same patient were present in both sets.
External validation was not performed; the models were only tested on the same ISIC-derived dataset used for training, limiting evidence of robustness across different imaging devices or populations.
The implementation relies on MATLAB R2020b and specific hardware (Intel i7-9750H, 16GB RAM), which may affect reproducibility and deployment feasibility in resource-constrained environments.
The claim of 'exceeding 99% accuracy' for KNN-PDNN is vague regarding which specific pretrained networks achieved this threshold, lacking granular performance breakdowns for all tested models.
